insight: Mobile Animus can be clicked the same turn as Vai Mauronax to convert red into Rhinos, providing temporary red flexibility; clicking on the turn after buying Vai is also viable but slightly worse due to inflation cost.
author: velizar_, shadourow
context: Discussion on timing and efficiency of Mobile Animus + Vai Mauronax conversion sequences.

insight: Rhino is undervalued relative to its cost; it's worth approximately 5 gold, possibly overpriced compared to Grimbotch which costs 5 and attacks one extra time.
author: velizar_, shadourow
context: Players evaluating unit value in context of Mobile Animus + Vai conversion strategy.

insight: Rhino absorbing prompt damage while attacking twice is a good defensive trade-off, especially against threats like Frostbite.
author: pikachumemes., amalloy
context: Discussion on actual value realized from Rhino in realistic game scenarios.

insight: With Flame Animus in the set, Player 2 can play T2 BB T3 Deadeye Operative (DEO) + Drone, which is very effective with other BB or BBR units and forces Player 1 to spend many turns defending.
author: recohou26799
context: Set-specific opening strategy discussion for Flame Animus sets.

insight: Against Deadeye Operative on Flame Animus sets, Player 1's decision to play T3 DD Savior depends on whether Doomed Wall exists; with Doomed Wall, follow with DD then DB; without it, just pursue economy.
author: recohou26799
context: Conditional opening responses to specific Deadeye Operative pressure.

insight: When buying Deadeye Operative, inflation is low, so Flame Animus availability doesn't matter much for many turns; standard Deadeye opening (P2: DD DD DBB 1DDE) should be used.
author: amalloy
context: Guidance on when Deadeye Operative is the correct pivot and how to sequence it.

insight: Early Deadeye Operative on low-inflation Flame Animus sets can pressure Player 1's tight gold economy, allowing follow-up with either Flame Wall or additional Deadeye + Drone to further pressure.
author: recohou26799
context: Tactical follow-ups to early Deadeye Operative tempo.

insight: Deadeye Operative can bridge tech transitions; for example, getting Deadeye into Xeno allows efficient progression between tech stages.
author: megasupp
context: General use of Deadeye Operative as transition unit.

insight: Xeno's best use is hard countering freeze effects, not just serving as a general absorber.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Clarification on Xeno's primary strength vs other absorption options.

insight: Xeno is best when you specifically want to spend 5GBB on a unit with Xeno's particular characteristics; its value is situational rather than universal.
author: amalloy
context: Expert perspective on Xeno's niche role.

insight: Thermite requires low inflation to purchase consistently; natural openings include base set plus Thermite for P1 (natural Animus) and P2 (DD DD DBA).
author: megasupp
context: Establishing baseline Thermite openings in sets where Thermite is good.

insight: If Thermite denies 2 absorb, it has paid for itself; don't feel bad switching to Walls as the game progresses.
author: megasupp
context: Value justification for Thermite investment.

insight: Thermite should not be spammed forever like Lanceteeth; eventually it becomes uncomfortable to keep buying them, so transition to attackers. However, opening with Thermite is usually mandatory for efficiency.
author: spiritfryer
context: Guidelines on Thermite purchase limits and transition timing.

insight: Mahar Rectifier is very expensive as a rushed unit and doesn't get value early against Wall or Tarsier defense; it requires opponent to have inflexible attack for optimal use.
author: doofmaster, velizar_
context: Discussion on early Mahar viability and defense matchups.

insight: Building 2 Mahars while skipping Blastforge is viable when Aegis is in the set with other good green units like Xaetron, allowing you to avoid early blue tech.
author: megasupp
context: Set-specific Mahar opening without blue tech.

insight: Protoplasm should not be purchased purely for absorption value in sets with good soak; it's only worthwhile to buy Proto to cycle Xaetron when the tempo gain yields more permanent attack.
author: jamberine, spiritfryer
context: Analysis of when Protoplasm cycling is economically justified.

insight: When good soak is available in a set, buying Protoplasm to cycle Xaetron is difficult to execute in practice despite being theoretically sound; the timing and turn efficiency issues make it awkward.
author: shadourow
context: Practical limitations on Protoplasm cycling strategies.

insight: The trade-off for buying Protoplasm (7GGR + 1 extra defense this turn) to get 4 attack and 4 defense next turn becomes less attractive when good soak is available, lowering the payoff value.
author: jamberine
context: Economic analysis of Protoplasm's value curve based on soak quality.

insight: When Xaetron cycling is not a core strategy due to good soak availability, buying multiple Animus instead becomes more efficient since there are fewer alternative resource sinks.
author: jamberine
context: Alternative econ path when Xaetron cycling is not optimal.

insight: Three engineers is not a viable default opening in Base Set Only (BSO); it should only be built when the random set specifically encourages it.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Correction of common beginner mistake about standard 3 engineer openers.

insight: Building green tech (Blastforge, etc.) has many justified reasons beyond Gauss Cannon; set-specific units can make green valuable.
author: pikachumemes.
context: General guidance against narrow thinking about tech choices.

insight: In sets with Rock-Paper-Scissors (RPS) dynamics, both players should make non-commital moves (buy more Drones) rather than committing to hard counters, until late game when one strategy emerges as dominant.
author: megasupp
context: Strategic guidance for RPS sets with multiple viable strategies.

insight: In RPS sets, Tatsu becomes the best strategy at high econ because you can afford tech while defending and buying multiple Tatsus; high econ breaks RPS.
author: megasupp
context: Explanation of how inflation and econ affect RPS set dynamics.

insight: Shredder is the best soak unit in sets containing it; players should consistently soak on Shredder after opponent reaches necessary damage thresholds.
author: sano712
context: Identifying and prioritizing best soak options.

insight: Preventing breach on expensive units like Centurion takes absolute priority; hold drones as soak/defense if necessary rather than risking a breach.
author: lyra1712
context: Critical defensive principle for protecting key units.

insight: Buying Rhinos for soak is inefficient when you have green resources available with no use; Forcefields should be preferred instead.
author: sano712
context: Optimization of soak unit choice based on available resources.

insight: Don't automatically follow wiki opening lines; understand the role and use cases of each unit, then apply that knowledge contextually rather than rote memorization.
author: sano712
context: Criticism of over-reliance on opening theory without understanding principles.

insight: Mahar Rectifier can be used as an alternating defense unit where two Mahars click back and forth, always absorbing 4, while also providing 2 threat on demand.
author: sano712
context: Advanced use of Mahar's dual absorb and threat capabilities.

insight: In sets with strong early red attackers and no good green absorption, aggressive red pressure (using Grimbotch, Rhinos, Tarsiers, Immolites) is often superior to slow econ strategies.
author: amalloy
context: Set-reading principle: strong early attackers encourage aggression over econ.

insight: When deciding between aggressive and econ strategies, evaluate what the set's key absorbers are; big slow absorbers like Doomed Mech and Omega Splitter support econ by forcing high spending on soak.
author: amalloy
context: Absorber role in determining whether early pressure or econ is preferable.

insight: Fourth Engineer is rarely correct; it should only be built when a unit like Vivid Drone specifically incentivizes it.
author: amalloy
context: General guidance on avoiding greedy 4-engineer openings.

insight: When buying a 4th Engineer, ensure your defense is still online; getting Wall up on time is critical, and 4th Engineer can delay Wall unprofitably.
author: amalloy
context: Specific downside of 4-engineer when it causes defense gaps.

insight: When you have a Doomed unit available for blocking, don't hold back drones to absorb if you already have proper defense up; click the Doomed unit to preserve Drones for econ.
author: amalloy
context: Resource optimization through proper use of Doomed absorbers.

insight: Buying Lanceteeth or Bloodrager without denying absorb is only worthwhile in sets with great prompt soak (like Aegis) where tempo has diminished value but permanent attack is also less valuable.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Situational analysis of prompt-attack units in high-soak sets.

insight: When evaluating whether to buy an attacker, consider opportunity cost: even in slow games, units like Gauss Charge can be worthwhile if they deny important defensive purchases.
author: pikachumemes.
context: General principle of evaluating denial value vs raw inflation metrics.
